steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Martinez and colleagues concluded that S. leucostigma ‘associates’ T. caesius alarm calls with danger. To support that conclusion, a finding must show the birds react specifically to those alarm calls — not to just any disturbance or sound.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — In some instances, *S. leucostigma* froze in place or scattered into vegetation when Martinez and colleagues approached but before they began playing sounds.: This shows the birds reacted to the researchers' approach, not to the alarm calls — it undermines the conclusion by suggesting a confounding cause. ✗
  • Choice B — When Martinez and colleagues played control sounds of random noise in the vicinity of *S. leucostigma*, the birds displayed no reaction.: A control test showing that random noise triggered nothing while T. caesius alarm calls triggered a defensive response proves the birds recognized those specific calls — exactly what ‘association’ requires. ✓
  • Choice C — Other bird species than *S. leucostigma* also showed a tendency to freeze in place or scatter into vegetation when Martinez and colleagues played *T. caesius* alarm calls.: This is about different species, not about whether S. leucostigma selectively recognizes T. caesius calls. It neither supports nor damages the conclusion about this species. ✗
  • Choice D — Martinez and colleagues played alarm calls from different *T. caesius* individuals and observed no significant variation in the responses of *S. leucostigma*.: Consistency across individual callers is interesting, but it does not show that the birds distinguish alarm calls from other sounds. ✗
That matches Choice B — When Martinez and colleagues played control sounds of random noise in the vicinity of *S. leucostigma*, the birds displayed no reaction..
